gh_142.hypothesis: The crash originates in `getRemotes` at `pkg/cmd/pr/create/create.go:1023`: when `gh pr create` is run in a freshly cloned fork with no `upstream` remote configured (and no `--repo` override), `opts.Remotes()` either returns an error that the function swallows because it is not unconditionally re-thrown, or returns a nil/empty `remotes` value with `err == nil`. In both paths the function returns `(remotes, nil)` — a nil/empty slice with no error — so the caller (the `Browser`/`BaseRef` resolution that asks for an "upstream" remote) dereferences a nil remote and produces the `runtime error: invalid memory address or nil pointer dereference` recorded in the trace at `pkg/cmd/pr/create.getRemotes(...)`. The Slack issue `iss_003` ("gh pr create just crashes if origin isn't set up right") reports the same crash in essentially the same scenario and is linked from gh_142, reinforcing that the failure mode is a missing empty/nil-remote guard in `getRemotes` rather than anything specific to a single user's git config.

evidence.label: gh pr create just crashes if origin isn't set up right (slack)
Original report — Panic on `gh pr create` when repo has no upstream remote

proposed_fix.rationale: Returning a clear error from `getRemotes` when no remotes are present prevents the caller from dereferencing a nil/empty remotes slice — the exact path the panic trace points at — and gives the user an actionable message instead of a stack trace.

gh_158.hypothesis: The root cause lives in the CLI's auth/token-refresh path: when an SSO-enforced org invalidates a stored token after the ~8-hour session window, the auth subsystem does not treat the resulting `HTTP 401: Bad credentials` as a refresh trigger and surfaces the error instead of calling `gh auth refresh` (or prompting the user to do so). The user is therefore forced to re-run `gh auth login` manually. The same behavior was reported previously as iss_007 (401 errors after being logged in for a few hours with SSO), which the issue record explicitly lists under `related_ids`, and a separate report (cli/cli#13709) indicates `gh auth refresh` itself is unreliable — together these point to the refresh-on-401 logic in the auth package (likely around the request middleware or token store that wraps API calls) as the component that fails to re-establish SSO-scoped credentials. The provided commit evidence matched on keywords only (coding-agent detection, AGENTS.md guidance, dev-tunnels bump) and does not bear on refresh behavior, so it is not cited.

evidence.label: Original report — Auth token not refreshed — 401 after SSO session expiry
Getting 401 errors after being logged in for a few hours (email)
`gh auth refresh --remove-scopes gist` has no effect (github)
